personality_voice.book_baseline: Book Harry is defined by a cluster of observable traits: quiet resilience under sustained abuse (he endures the Dursleys with dry internal commentary rather than self-pity), dry humor deployed as a coping mechanism and as a weapon against bullies ('The poor toilet's never had anything as horrible as your head down it'), instinctive moral courage that overrides self-interest (jumping on the troll, confronting Malfoy over Neville's Remembrall), genuine intellectual curiosity about the wizarding world (he asks constant questions, reads his school books eagerly, connects the Gringotts break-in to vault 713 himself), and a persistent insecurity about his own worth and competence ('I bet I'm the worst in the class,' 'I think you must have made a mistake, I don't think I can be a wizard'). His voice is direct, occasionally sarcastic, and notably unimpressed by his own fame. He is also capable of obsessive fixation — the Mirror of Erised sequence shows him returning three nights in a row against Ron's advice, driven by grief he cannot articulate. He is not passive: he initiates, argues, disobeys, and acts on instinct.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film preserves the core traits well. Harry's dry humor survives in small moments ('Anytime' to the snake, the grin at the end about threatening Dudley). His instinctive courage is shown in the troll scene, the Quidditch confrontation with Malfoy, and the final descent into the trapdoor. His insecurity about his own abilities is present in 'I'm not going home. Not really' and his hesitation before the Sorting Hat. His curiosity is shown through questions to Hagrid and his reading of the Gringotts article. The Mirror of Erised scene is faithfully rendered emotionally. His directness with Malfoy ('I think I can tell the wrong sort for myself, thanks') is intact.
personality_voice.difference: Several secondary traits are compressed or lost. The book's sustained internal monologue — which reveals Harry's dry, self-aware humor and his habit of noticing absurdity — is necessarily absent in film, but the film does not replace it with equivalent behavioral signals in many scenes. His obsessive return to the mirror (three nights, defying Ron's explicit warning) is reduced to two visits with less emphasis on the compulsive quality. His intellectual initiative — connecting the Gringotts break-in to vault 713 himself while at Hagrid's — is preserved in the film but slightly compressed. His voice in the film is somewhat more reactive and less verbally sharp than in the books; the book Harry has more wit in dialogue (the Dudley toilet insult, the 'I know some things, I can do math and stuff' response to Hagrid). The film Harry is slightly more wide-eyed and less sardonic.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The main traits — courage, insecurity about worth, curiosity, dry humor, moral instinct — are preserved and evidenced. The loss is in secondary texture: the sustained internal irony, the more pronounced verbal sharpness, and the compulsive quality of the mirror obsession. This warrants a minor deduction but not a heavy one, as the character remains recognizable and the dominant pattern is faithful.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Harry is the primary agent of the plot. He initiates the investigation into the Sorcerer's Stone (connecting the Gringotts break-in to vault 713 at Hagrid's hut), proposes the plan to go through the trapdoor, makes the key decisions in each chamber (playing the flute for Fluffy, identifying the winged key, directing the chess strategy with Ron, choosing to send Hermione back and go on alone), and ultimately defeats Quirrell through his own physical contact. He also acts independently in smaller moments: confronting Malfoy over the Remembrall, going to the forest after Snape, overhearing Snape and Quirrell. His agency is consistent and causal throughout.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: Harry's agency is largely preserved. He initiates the trapdoor plan, identifies the winged key, makes the decision to go on alone, and defeats Quirrell. The forest scene where he follows Snape is retained. His confrontation with Malfoy over the Remembrall is intact. He connects the dragon egg stranger to Hagrid's secret about Fluffy.
narrative_role_agency.difference: Two meaningful transfers occur. First, in the books Harry himself connects the Gringotts break-in to vault 713 while reading the newspaper at Hagrid's hut — this is a moment of independent deduction. In the film, Harry reads the article aloud to Ron but the connection to Hagrid's visit is made more explicitly as a group realization rather than Harry's solo insight. Second, and more significantly, the chess game in the film is directed entirely by Ron with Harry and Hermione as passive pieces; in the books Ron also directs, but Harry's role as a bishop who moves on Ron's instruction is the same — this is not a transfer. However, in the books Harry proposes going through the trapdoor that night with more explicit reasoning ('It's tonight... Snape's going through the trapdoor tonight'), which the film preserves. The Hermione-sends-Snape's-robes-on-fire moment is the same in both. No major agency transfer occurs; the reductions are minor.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: Harry's role as primary agent is intact. The minor compression of his independent deductive moments does not rise to the level of a full transfer. A small deduction is warranted for the slight flattening of his investigative initiative.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Harry's motivations in Book 1 are layered. Surface motivation: survive, fit in, learn magic. Deeper motivations: find belonging (he has never had a family or friends), understand his own identity and history (he knows almost nothing about his parents or himself), and prove his worth (he fears being the worst in the class, fears being a fraud). The internal conflict is most visible in two places: (1) the Mirror of Erised, where his deepest desire — family — overrides his rational judgment and pulls him back three nights running against Ron's advice; (2) his response to being told he is famous, which produces anxiety rather than pleasure ('Everyone thinks I'm special... but I don't know anything about magic at all'). There is also a conflict between his instinct to act and his awareness that acting gets him into trouble — he repeatedly breaks rules while knowing the consequences.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The film preserves the core motivations. The Mirror of Erised scene captures his longing for family clearly. His anxiety about fame and competence is present in the Leaky Cauldron scene and the conversation with Hagrid at the station. His rule-breaking instinct is shown throughout. The final line — 'I'm not going home. Not really' — captures his sense of belonging at Hogwarts.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The internal conflict around the mirror is compressed. In the books, Harry returns a third night alone, explicitly against Ron's warning, driven by a compulsion he cannot fully explain — this is the clearest dramatization of his internal conflict between desire and reason. The film shows two visits but does not emphasize the compulsive, almost addictive quality of the third solo return. The anxiety about being a fraud ('I think you must have made a mistake') is present in the film but less sustained — in the books it recurs across multiple scenes (the train, Hagrid's hut, the first Potions class). The film also omits the internal monologue during the Sorting Hat scene where Harry actively thinks 'Not Slytherin' — though this line is preserved in dialogue, the book's internal reasoning about why (his fear of being associated with dark wizards) is absent.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: Motivations are simplified but still correct. The core desires — belonging, identity, proving worth — are present. The internal conflict is present but less sustained and less dramatized than in the books. A moderate deduction is warranted.

character_arc.book_baseline: Harry's arc in Book 1 moves from ignorance and powerlessness to knowledge and agency. Starting point: he knows nothing about his identity, has no friends, no family connection, no sense of worth, and is physically and socially dominated by the Dursleys. Key turning points: learning he is a wizard (identity); arriving at Hogwarts and finding belonging for the first time; discovering his natural talent on a broomstick (competence); the Mirror of Erised (confronting his deepest wound — the absence of family); the final confrontation with Quirrell/Voldemort (acting on courage despite fear). End point: he has found belonging, demonstrated courage, and accepted that Voldemort will return — but he faces it with equanimity rather than terror. The arc also includes a small but important beat: his decision to use the invisibility cloak alone the first time, keeping it from Ron, signals growing individuation. The final line about threatening Dudley signals he has internalized a new sense of power and identity.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film follows the same arc structure. The key turning points are all present: the revelation of his identity, arrival at Hogwarts, the broomstick discovery, the Mirror of Erised, the final confrontation. The ending — 'I'm not going home. Not really' — captures the arc's resolution of belonging. The Hagrid exchange about threatening Dudley is preserved.
character_arc.difference: The arc is compressed in two ways. First, the Mirror of Erised arc — which in the books spans three nights and includes a solo return that dramatizes Harry's internal struggle — is reduced to two visits, slightly weakening the emotional weight of Dumbledore's intervention. Second, the book's arc includes a sustained period of social ostracism after the points loss (Harry goes from hero to most-hated student), which forces him to confront the fragility of his new belonging and to recommit to acting on principle rather than popularity. This is entirely absent from the film, removing a meaningful step in his arc. The film also omits his offer to resign from the Quidditch team — a moment that shows his moral seriousness about the consequences of his actions.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The arc is compressed but still faithful in its major beats. The missing ostracism sequence and the reduced mirror arc remove important steps that deepen the arc's emotional logic. A moderate deduction is warranted — the arc is recognizable but incomplete.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Three relationships define Harry in Book 1. (1) Harry-Hagrid: Hagrid is Harry's first genuine adult ally, the person who gives Harry his identity and his first experience of being cared for. The dynamic is warm, protective, and slightly unequal — Hagrid is emotionally open in ways Harry is not yet. (2) Harry-Ron: an immediate, easy friendship built on mutual recognition of outsider status; Ron is Harry's first peer friend, and their dynamic involves genuine reciprocity — Harry shares his sweets, Ron shares his world. (3) Harry-Hermione: begins as friction (Harry finds her bossy and interfering) and shifts to genuine friendship after the troll incident, with Harry recognizing her courage and loyalty. The Dursley relationship is defined by sustained cruelty and Harry's quiet endurance.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: All three key relationships are present and their dynamics are broadly preserved. The Harry-Hagrid warmth is evident in multiple scenes. The Harry-Ron friendship develops naturally on the train. The Harry-Hermione arc from friction to friendship is shown, though compressed. The Dursley dynamic is established in the opening sequences.
key_relationships.difference: The Harry-Hermione friction is compressed — the film omits the extended period where Harry and Ron actively dislike her (Ron's 'she's a nightmare' line is present but the sustained mutual avoidance is not). The Harry-Hagrid relationship loses some texture: the book includes Harry noticing that Hagrid avoids his eyes when asked about Snape, and Harry's growing awareness that Hagrid is not telling him everything — a dynamic of trust complicated by partial information. This is partially preserved in the film (Hagrid's 'I shouldn't have said that' moments) but less nuanced. The Harry-Dursley relationship is faithfully rendered in its broad strokes but the book's sustained internal commentary on the dynamic — Harry's dry acceptance of his own mistreatment — is absent.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: Key relationships are mostly faithful with some compression. The emotional dynamics are recognizable. The main loss is in the Harry-Hermione friction arc and the Harry-Hagrid complexity around partial trust. A small deduction is warranted.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Book Harry has several layers of complexity that resist simple summary. (1) He is simultaneously famous and ignorant — the gap between his reputation and his self-knowledge is a source of genuine anxiety, not false modesty. (2) He is capable of moral ambiguity: he wants to curse Dudley, he feels guilty about Mrs. Figg's broken leg only because it means he gets to go to the zoo, he is pleased when Dudley gets a pig's tail. (3) He has a compulsive streak — the mirror obsession, the midnight duel he knows is a trap but cannot resist because of Malfoy's face 'looming up out of the darkness.' (4) He is socially isolated in a way that shapes his behavior: his generosity with sweets on the train is explicitly linked to never having had anything to share before. (5) The ostracism arc after the points loss shows him experiencing the cost of his own choices and making a genuine moral recommitment. (6) His response to learning about his parents is complex — grief, curiosity, and a kind of hunger that the mirror makes visible.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film preserves the fame-ignorance gap and the grief around his parents. His generosity on the train is shown. His compulsive streak is partially shown in the mirror visits. His moral instinct is shown in the troll and Malfoy scenes.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: Several nuances are lost or reduced. The moral ambiguity around small selfishnesses (Mrs. Figg, wanting to curse Dudley) is absent — the film Harry is more straightforwardly good-natured. The compulsive quality of the mirror obsession is reduced. The ostracism arc — which is the most important complexity beat in the second half of the book, showing Harry experiencing the social cost of his choices and having to recommit to principle — is entirely absent from the film. The book's sustained internal voice, which provides ironic distance and self-awareness, has no equivalent in the film. The midnight duel sequence, which shows Harry acting against his own better judgment because he cannot resist Malfoy, is present in the film but without the internal conflict that makes it revealing. The book also includes Harry's growing suspicion that Hagrid is withholding information — a complexity in his relationship with his most trusted adult — which is only partially preserved.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The film preserves the broad characterization but loses the moral ambiguity, the compulsive streak, the ostracism arc, and the sustained internal irony that give book Harry his texture. These are not trivial omissions — the ostracism arc in particular is a meaningful step in his development that the film skips entirely. A moderate deduction is warranted.

lost_or_transferred_material: The sustained social ostracism arc after Harry loses 150 points for Gryffindor — going from hero to most-hated student — is entirely absent from the film
Harry's offer to resign from the Quidditch team as a moral response to having cost Gryffindor points
Harry's third solo return to the Mirror of Erised, explicitly against Ron's warning, which dramatizes his compulsive grief
Harry's small moral ambiguities: pleasure at Mrs. Figg's broken leg, wanting to curse Dudley, satisfaction at Dudley getting a pig's tail
Harry's sustained internal ironic commentary on the Dursleys and his own situation throughout the early chapters
Harry's growing awareness that Hagrid is withholding information (noticing Hagrid won't meet his eyes when asked about Snape)
The midnight duel sequence's internal conflict — Harry knowing it is a trap but being unable to resist because of Malfoy's face
Harry's explicit anxiety about being the worst in the class, which recurs across multiple scenes in the books but is compressed in the film
The book's extended Harry-Hermione friction period before the troll incident, which makes their eventual friendship more earned
Harry's independent deduction connecting the Gringotts break-in to vault 713 while reading the newspaper at Hagrid's hut — slightly more group-mediated in the film
score_caps_applied: No hard caps applied. Total of 79 falls within acceptable range given the level of compression and lost material. Cap 1 (85) is not triggered as the remaining material is substantially faithful. Cap 3 (75) is not triggered as no core agency beat is fully transferred to another character.
key_observations: The film adaptation of Harry Potter in Philosopher's Stone is substantially faithful to the books. The core identity — a resilient, curious, instinctively brave boy who is simultaneously famous and ignorant, longing for family and belonging — is preserved and evidenced throughout. The major plot beats, key relationships, and arc structure are all present. The primary losses are in texture and interiority: the sustained internal irony that gives book Harry his voice, the moral ambiguities that complicate his goodness, the compulsive quality of his grief at the mirror, and most significantly the ostracism arc that forces him to confront the cost of his own choices. The film Harry is slightly more reactive, slightly more straightforwardly heroic, and somewhat less sardonic than his book counterpart. These are meaningful compressions but not distortions — the character is recognizable, his agency is intact, and his emotional core is faithfully rendered. A score of 79 reflects a faithful but simplified adaptation that preserves the essential character while losing the secondary texture that makes him fully complex.
